content.text: model is a cognitive architecture for rational agents, introduced by Michael Bratman (1987) and formalised for AI by Rao and Georgeff (1995). It models the agent's mental state with three components that mirror how humans deliberate and act:
— the agent's (possibly incomplete or uncertain) knowledge about the world.
— the motivational states: what the agent wants to achieve. Desires can be inconsistent.
— the subset of desires the agent has committed to act upon. Intentions constrain future deliberation (the agent will not abandon them without reason).
BDI is not just an implementation pattern — it is a
: how a resource-bounded agent should decide what to do next in a dynamic world.

attrs.alt: BDI Agent Architecture: Beliefs, Desires, Intentions with deliberation and means-end reasoning
attrs.title: BDI Agent Architecture

content.text: selects a plan from the library to execute the intention. Actions flow back to the environment, which generates new percepts that update beliefs.
The agent class combines the three components with the
: perceive → deliberate → adopt intentions → execute action:
A warehouse robot uses BDI to pick up and deliver parcels. The plan library holds pre-compiled procedures; desires specify the goals; beliefs track where the robot and parcel are:

# Build a delivery robot using the BDI framework

def has_parcel(beliefs: set[Belief]) -> bool:
    return any(b.predicate == "carrying" and "parcel" in b.args for b in beliefs)

def at_destination(beliefs: set[Belief]) -> bool:
    return any(b.predicate == "location" and b.args == ("robot", "warehouse") for b in beliefs)

def at_pickup(beliefs: set[Belief]) -> bool:
    return any(b.predicate == "location" and b.args == ("robot", "bay_3") for b in beliefs)


# Plans (pre-compiled if-then procedures)
fetch_plan = Plan(
    name="fetch_parcel",
    trigger=lambda b: not has_parcel(b),  # applicable when not carrying
    steps=["navigate_to_bay_3", "pickup_parcel"],
)
deliver_plan = Plan(
    name="deliver_parcel",
    trigger=has_parcel,  # applicable when carrying
    steps=["navigate_to_warehouse", "drop_parcel"],
)

# Desires
pick_up_desire = Desire(
    goal="pick up parcel",
    condition=has_parcel,  # satisfied when carrying
    priority=8,
)
deliver_desire = Desire(
    goal="deliver parcel",
    condition=at_destination,  # satisfied when at warehouse
    priority=9,
)

agent = BDIAgent(plan_library=[fetch_plan, deliver_plan])
agent.desires = [pick_up_desire, deliver_desire]

# Simulate 4 cycles
initial_percepts = [
    Belief("location", ("robot", "bay_3")),
    Belief("parcel_available", ("bay_3",)),
]
env: dict = {}
for i in range(4):
    action = agent.step(initial_percepts if i == 0 else [], env)
    print(f"Cycle {i+1}: {action}")
# Output:
# Cycle 1: navigate_to_bay_3
# Cycle 2: pickup_parcel
# Cycle 3: navigate_to_warehouse
# Cycle 4: drop_parcel
Real environments produce failures: routes are blocked, objects are missing, actuators jam. A BDI agent handles these by
and either switching to a fallback plan or re-deliberating. This keeps the architecture clean — failure handling is encoded in the plan library, not scattered through the reasoning loop:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable


@dataclass
class PlanWithFallback:
    name: str
    trigger: Callable
    steps: list[str]
    fallback: "PlanWithFallback | None" = None


class RobustBDIAgent(BDIAgent):
    """Extends BDIAgent with plan failure handling."""

    def __init__(self, plan_library: list[PlanWithFallback]):
        super().__init__(plan_library)
        self._failed_steps: set[str] = set()

    def report_failure(self, action: str) -> None:
        """Called by the environment when an action fails."""
        self._failed_steps.add(action)
        # Drop intention that called this action and try fallback
        if self.intentions:
            failed_intention = self.intentions[0]
            plan = failed_intention.plan
            if hasattr(plan, "fallback") and plan.fallback is not None:
                # Replace current plan with fallback
                self.intentions[0] = Intention(
                    failed_intention.desire,
                    plan.fallback,
                )
                print(f"Switching to fallback plan: {plan.fallback.name}")
            else:
                # No fallback — abandon intention
                self.intentions.pop(0)
                print(f"Abandoned intention: {failed_intention.desire.goal}")


# Example: primary route blocked, use service lift instead
service_lift_plan = PlanWithFallback(
    name="use_service_lift",
    trigger=lambda b: True,
    steps=["navigate_to_service_lift", "take_lift", "navigate_to_warehouse"],
)
primary_delivery_plan = PlanWithFallback(
    name="direct_delivery",
    trigger=lambda b: True,
    steps=["navigate_to_warehouse", "drop_parcel"],
    fallback=service_lift_plan,
)
A key design decision in BDI is
the agent remains to an intention when the world changes. Three classical strategies:
When the triggering condition is no longer true
Dynamic environments, re-plan on world change
When goal is no longer achievable or relevant
Because BDI agents have three distinct mental states, unit tests target each independently:

import pytest


def test_believe_update_replaces_old_fact():
    agent = BDIAgent([])
    b1 = Belief("location", ("robot", "room_A"))
    b2 = Belief("location", ("robot", "room_B"))
    agent.perceive(b1)
    agent.perceive(b2)
    locs = {b.args[1] for b in agent.beliefs if b.predicate == "location"}
    # Both b1 and b2 have same predicate+args key — b2 should replace b1
    # (predicate="location", args=("robot", ...) differs so both present here)
    assert b2 in agent.beliefs


def test_deliberate_sorts_by_priority():
    agent = BDIAgent([])
    low = Desire("low", lambda b: False, priority=2)
    high = Desire("high", lambda b: False, priority=9)
    agent.desires = [low, high]
    result = agent.deliberate()
    assert result[0] is high


def test_satisfied_desire_excluded_from_deliberation():
    agent = BDIAgent([])
    already_done = Desire("done", lambda b: True, priority=10)
    pending = Desire("pending", lambda b: False, priority=5)
    agent.desires = [already_done, pending]
    result = agent.deliberate()
    assert already_done not in result
    assert pending in result


def test_full_bdi_cycle_executes_plan_steps():
    plan = Plan("test_plan", trigger=lambda b: True, steps=["step_1", "step_2"])
    desire = Desire("do_test", condition=lambda b: False, priority=5)
    agent = BDIAgent([plan])
    agent.desires = [desire]

    action1 = agent.step([], {})
    action2 = agent.step([], {})
    action3 = agent.step([], {})
    assert action1 == "step_1"
    assert action2 == "step_2"
    assert action3 is None  # plan exhausted
that should not be abandoned after every world change.
: situations can be classified into a manageable set of plan-triggering contexts.
: a log of intentions and plan switches is far clearer than a neural policy.
when the state space is too large for a hand-crafted plan library, or when the environment is fully learnable from data — use RL or LLM-backed planning there.

BDI separates what the agent knows (beliefs), what it wants (desires), and what it has committed to do (intentions). The deliberation step filters desires into intentions; means-end reasoning maps intentions to plans from a pre-compiled library. Commitment strategies determine when an agent revises its intentions in response to world changes — the fundamental tension in any practical reasoning system. The pattern is highly auditable, handles failure gracefully through fallback plans, and scales well to multi-step tasks in partially predictable environments.
